title: Vaccinating sheep with a clostridial vaccine
subtitle: The annual jab that prevents the most common sudden deaths in sheep.
excerpt: Clostridial diseases kill sheep quickly and without obvious warning. The vaccine is inexpensive, the injection straightforward, and the annual programme covers seven or eight different diseases in one product. How to run the syringe, where to inject, and what records to keep.
sourceNotes: DEFRA/APHA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Sheep (2021). AHDB Beef and Lamb, vaccination guide for sheep producers (2023). British Veterinary Association (BVA), sheep disease prevention guidance. Merck Veterinary Manual, clostridial diseases of sheep.

glossaryTerms.definition: An injection programme that protects sheep against a group of diseases caused by Clostridium bacteria, including pulpy kidney, tetanus, lamb dysentery, and others. Requires a primary course of two injections followed by annual boosters.
An annual repeat injection given to a sheep that has already completed the primary two-injection course. Maintains the immunity established by the primary course.

The first milk produced by a ewe after lambing, rich in antibodies. Ewes vaccinated 4 to 6 weeks before lambing produce colostrum containing clostridial antibodies that protect newborn lambs in their first weeks of life.
Also called pulpy kidney disease. A clostridial disease that kills rapidly, typically in well-fed young lambs or sheep on lush pasture. The first visible sign is often a dead sheep.

content.text: The
clostridial vaccination

programme is one of the most cost-effective things a sheep keeper does. Products such as Heptavac-P (or its generic equivalents) cover seven or eight clostridial diseases plus pasteurellosis in a single injection. The annual
booster
given to pregnant ewes 4 to 6 weeks before lambing also loads their
colostrum
with antibodies. These protect newborn lambs before their own immune system is functional.
Why vaccinate
Clostridial diseases such as
enterotoxaemia

, lamb dysentery, tetanus, and braxy are the most common reasons for finding a sheep dead with no prior warning signs. They are caused by Clostridium bacteria that are present in soil and in the gut of healthy sheep. A sudden dietary change, a period of high-energy feed, or a wound provides the trigger for rapid toxin production. The vaccine does not eliminate the bacteria; it prevents the immune system from being overwhelmed when the trigger occurs.
The vaccination calendar
New stock, or sheep with an unknown vaccination history, need the primary course: two injections 4 to 6 weeks apart. After that, a single annual booster is sufficient to maintain protection.

For autumn-lambing flocks: give the annual booster in late summer so it falls 4 to 6 weeks before the first ewes are due. For spring-lambing flocks, booster in October or November for February and March lambing dates. Lambs born to vaccinated ewes gain passive protection from colostrum but need their own primary course starting at 6 to 8 weeks of age.
What you need
A licensed clostridial vaccine (prescription product in the UK; obtain from your vet)
A 5 ml repeating vaccination syringe with 18-gauge disposable needles
A handling pen or race to present each sheep safely
Holding register to record which animals were vaccinated and the product batch number

attrs.title: Keep the vaccine cold and shake before each use
attrs.body: Clostridial vaccines are adjuvanted oil emulsions and need shaking before each draw to ensure the dose is consistent. Store at 2 to 8°C and do not let them freeze; a frozen-then-thawed vaccine loses efficacy. Check the expiry date before opening; an opened bottle kept cold remains usable for the day but discard any that is not used within the session.

content.text: The injection procedure

Subcutaneous (under the skin) injection is the standard route for clostridial vaccines. The recommended site is the neck or the axilla (armpit), well behind the elbow on the side of the chest. Avoid the rump and hindquarters; a reaction at the injection site can cause a lump that reduces the carcase value of a meat animal.

Part the fleece with the fingers of one hand. Pinch a loose fold of skin between thumb and forefinger and push the needle into the tent of skin, angled away from the body. The needle should move easily; resistance means you have hit muscle. Depress the plunger and withdraw. A small lump confirms the dose went under the skin rather than into muscle.
Change the needle between every 10 animals, or immediately if the needle touches the ground or a contaminated surface. A blunt needle causes more pain and increases injection site reaction.
Records

Record the date, product name, batch number, expiry date, number of animals treated, and the name of the person who administered the vaccine in the holding register. UK sheep welfare codes require a medicine record; your vet needs the batch number if there is a vaccine reaction outbreak.

items.cause: Normal adjuvant reaction: the oil in the vaccine causes brief localised swelling
items.fix: Most lumps resolve within 3 to 4 weeks. Contact your vet if a lump is hot, rapidly growing, or the sheep is off-colour. Abscess formation at the site is uncommon but possible.

items.cause: Rare anaphylactic shock: can occur in animals previously exposed to the vaccine
items.fix: Keep adrenaline solution nearby when vaccinating. Call your vet immediately. This needs an emergency injection from the vet.

items.symptom: Stock still dying from clostridial disease despite vaccination
items.cause: Primary course not completed, booster missed, cold-chain failure, or vaccination given too close to the risk event
items.fix: Check your records against the product datasheet's recommended timing. A primary course needs two injections to establish immunity. A single injection does not protect. Review the cold-chain and storage with your vet.
